sections.plain_summary: IgG food sensitivity refers to elevated IgG antibodies in the blood against specific foods. These reactions are delayed — symptoms can appear hours or even days after eating a trigger food — which makes them extremely difficult to identify without testing. Unlike IgE-mediated allergies, which cause immediate and often dramatic reactions, IgG responses are subtle, chronic, and cumulative. Common complaints include bloating, brain fog, fatigue, skin problems, and joint pain. The science around IgG testing is genuinely contested: mainstream allergists largely dismiss it, while many functional medicine practitioners and patients report meaningful improvement after acting on results. Understanding the controversy — and what the evidence does and does not support — is essential for anyone investigating this as a possible driver of their symptoms.
sections.detailed_summary:
IgG antibodies are a normal part of the immune system's response to food proteins that cross the gut lining. Some researchers and clinicians argue that elevated IgG4 antibodies in particular may reflect a tolerance mechanism rather than a pathological reaction, meaning the body has simply been exposed to that food frequently. Others — particularly in the functional and integrative medicine world — argue that elevated IgG antibodies, especially IgG1–3, can reflect immune activation in the gut wall, and that when combined with increased intestinal permeability ('leaky gut'), these reactions may contribute to chronic systemic inflammation and a wide range of symptoms.

The mainstream allergology position, supported by the American Academy of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology, is that IgG food sensitivity testing has no validated diagnostic role and should not be used to guide elimination diets. Critics point out that: (1) healthy people with no symptoms also have elevated food-specific IgG antibodies; (2) the tests lack standardisation across laboratories; and (3) elimination based on IgG results can lead to unnecessarily restrictive diets.

However, a significant number of people — particularly those with IBS, eczema, migraines, and chronic fatigue who have not responded to conventional treatment — report substantial symptom reduction after eliminating their IgG-flagged foods for 4–8 weeks. Several randomised controlled trials in IBS populations have found that IgG-guided elimination diets outperformed sham diets on symptom scores, lending some empirical support to the approach even without full mechanistic understanding.

The likely reality sits between these positions: IgG elevation may be a marker of gut barrier dysfunction and immune activation rather than the direct cause of symptoms. In that framing, IgG test results are one imperfect signal pointing toward foods that the immune system is reacting to — possibly because the gut lining is compromised, possibly because of high intake frequency, or both. Acting on those results through a structured elimination and reintroduction protocol, while simultaneously addressing gut health, may help some people identify personal triggers and reduce their symptom burden.

This is a condition where self-directed investigation, guided by an informed practitioner, can be genuinely productive. People researching IgG food sensitivities are typically doing so because conventional medicine has not provided answers — and that research, when paired with careful dietary experimentation and good clinical oversight, often leads to meaningful insight.

prevalence.summary: IgG food antibodies are detectable in virtually everyone; what percentage experience clinically meaningful symptoms from them is unknown and contested.
prevalence.estimate: Up to 45% of the general population may report some form of food intolerance or sensitivity; IgG-guided elimination studies suggest 10–30% of people with IBS-type symptoms see significant benefit from acting on IgG results.
prevalence.notes: There are no rigorous epidemiological estimates for 'IgG food sensitivity' as a clinical entity because it lacks a standardised diagnostic definition. Commercial IgG testing varies substantially between labs, making cross-study comparison difficult. Women are more frequently tested than men, partly reflecting higher rates of help-seeking for chronic gut and systemic symptoms. The contested diagnostic status means the condition is almost certainly underreported in clinical databases.

subgroups.name: Gut-dominant responders
subgroups.description: Individuals whose primary IgG-driven symptoms are gastrointestinal — bloating, cramping, altered bowel habits, nausea. Often overlap significantly with IBS.
distinguishing_features: Symptoms closely track food intake with a 2–24 hour delay
IgG reactivity often skews toward high-frequency dietary staples (wheat, dairy, eggs)
Frequently have co-existing gut dysbiosis or SIBO

subgroups.name: Neuro-cognitive responders
subgroups.description: Individuals whose main complaints are brain fog, fatigue, headaches, or mood changes rather than gut symptoms.
distinguishing_features: Gut symptoms may be absent or minimal despite food reactions
Symptom onset often 4–24 hours post-ingestion, making dietary tracking very difficult
May have elevated zonulin or signs of intestinal permeability
Sometimes overlaps with MCAS or chronic fatigue presentations

subgroups.name: Skin-dominant responders
subgroups.description: Individuals presenting primarily with eczema, urticaria, acne, or psoriasis flares linked to dietary patterns.
distinguishing_features: Skin reactions typically appear 12–48 hours after trigger food
Dairy, eggs, gluten, and soy are commonly flagged
Often have concurrent atopic history or personal/family history of atopic dermatitis

subgroups.name: High-load / cumulative responders
subgroups.description: Individuals who tolerate individual reactive foods in small quantities but experience symptoms when multiple trigger foods are consumed in the same day or meal.
distinguishing_features: Inconsistent symptom pattern that confuses standard elimination approaches
Often described as having a 'threshold' or 'bucket' effect
Testing may reveal a broad panel of moderate-level reactions rather than a few high-level ones

subgroups.name: Leaky-gut primary
subgroups.description: Individuals where intestinal permeability appears to be the upstream driver, causing widespread low-grade IgG reactions across many foods rather than a few specific ones.
distinguishing_features: IgG panel shows reactions to a large number of foods (sometimes 20+)
Broad multi-system symptoms including fatigue, joint pain, and mood disturbance
May have identifiable upstream causes: prior antibiotic use, SIBO, NSAID overuse, chronic stress
Restricting all flagged foods rarely works long-term; gut repair is needed first

symptoms.notes: Typically delayed 2–24 hours after eating the trigger food; often described as 'food baby' bloating unrelated to volume consumed
May alternate with constipation; overlaps significantly with IBS presentation
Less frequently recognised as a food sensitivity symptom; may occur in isolation or alternating with diarrhoea
Often mistaken for gastroparesis or functional dyspepsia; may be worse with specific trigger foods
Can be diffuse or localised; typically not severe enough to be mistaken for surgical emergency
Often described as 'thinking through mud'; can be more prominent than gut symptoms in some individuals
May be profound and disproportionate to sleep quality; often worse in the hours after a reactive meal
Delayed onset (up to 48 hours post-ingestion) makes dietary connection hard to identify without a diary
Particularly reported with gluten and dairy reactivity; may worsen morning stiffness
IgG-related skin flares typically appear 12–48 hours after trigger food; distinct from immediate IgE urticaria
Dairy and high-glycaemic foods are most commonly implicated in research; IgG testing sometimes reveals additional triggers
Chronic congestion (not seasonal) that clears on elimination of trigger foods, most commonly dairy
Gut-brain axis involvement may mediate mood effects; often dismissed by patients as unrelated to diet
Poor sleep quality without obvious cause; may improve with elimination of reactive foods
Facial puffiness on waking or general water retention; some patients notice this resolves quickly on elimination
Diffuse, chronic aching without obvious cause; may be part of a broader inflammatory picture

key_patterns: Symptoms are delayed (2–48 hours after eating), making real-time cause-and-effect difficult to identify
Symptoms affect multiple body systems simultaneously (gut + brain fog + skin + fatigue), suggesting a systemic immune or inflammatory process
Chronic symptoms that correlate poorly with identified IgE allergies or obvious intolerances (e.g., lactase deficiency)
Symptoms improve significantly during periods of dietary restriction (travel, illness, fasting) without the person realising food was the variable
A 'rotating' pattern where symptoms seem random but actually track high-frequency foods consumed in the previous day or two
IgG test showing high reactivity to frequently eaten foods (wheat, dairy, eggs, corn) — particularly plausible as markers of gut permeability

at_home.name: Structured elimination and reintroduction diary
at_home.description: Remove the most commonly reactive foods (wheat/gluten, dairy, eggs, soy, corn) simultaneously for 4–6 weeks, keeping a detailed symptom diary with dates, times, foods eaten, and symptom scores. After the elimination phase, reintroduce one food at a time every 3–5 days, eating a meaningful serving twice daily and recording any symptom changes over the following 48–72 hours. The reintroduction phase is where the most specific information is generated.
at_home.safety_notes: Elimination should not be extended beyond 8 weeks without professional guidance, as nutritional gaps can develop. Not appropriate for those with a history of eating disorders without dietitian oversight. People who are underweight or already eating a highly restricted diet should not begin elimination without professional support.

at_home.name: Symptom and food diary (pre-testing baseline)
at_home.description: Before attempting elimination, keep a detailed 2-week diary recording every food and drink consumed, timing, quantity, and symptom type and severity. Rate symptoms on a consistent scale (0–10). Look for 24–48 hour lag patterns rather than immediate reactions. This baseline is invaluable for any subsequent practitioner consultation and for identifying the most suspicious foods before formal testing.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe for anyone. Most people underestimate how much information a well-kept diary generates — the structure is what makes it useful.

at_home.name: Pulse test (informal provocation)
at_home.description: An informal (not clinically validated) method sometimes used by naturopaths: measure resting pulse, eat a single suspected food, and measure pulse again at 15 and 30 minutes. A rise of more than 10 beats per minute is sometimes interpreted as a sign of reaction. This is not reliable or evidence-based but may help some people identify obvious reactions as part of a broader investigative process.
at_home.safety_notes: Not validated; should not replace structured elimination. Do not use for suspected IgE-mediated (immediate) allergies.

clinical.name: IgG food antibody panel (serum)
clinical.description: A blood test measuring IgG antibody levels (typically IgG1–4) against a panel of 90–200+ foods. Results are usually provided as low/moderate/high reactivity. Some labs test all four IgG subclasses; others test only total IgG4. Methodology varies widely between commercial labs (ELISA-based), which affects reproducibility and comparability of results. Not currently endorsed by mainstream allergy bodies but widely offered by functional medicine practitioners and some integrative GPs.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine doctors, integrative GPs, naturopaths, and some direct-to-consumer labs. Labs commonly used include Cyrex Array 10, US BioTek, Dunwoody Labs, and YorkTest (UK). Some NHS/private allergists may order it on request but may not consider results actionable. Cost typically ranges from £150–£500 / $200–$600 depending on the panel breadth.

clinical.name: IgG4-specific food panel
clinical.description: A variant of the standard IgG panel that focuses specifically on IgG4 subclass antibodies. IgG4 is associated with repeated antigen exposure and is sometimes argued to represent tolerance rather than active sensitivity — making the interpretation of high IgG4 levels particularly contested. Some researchers argue IgG4 elevation specifically flags clinically meaningful reactions; others argue the opposite. Context from symptoms and elimination response matters more than the number alone.
clinical.availability: Available through specialist functional and integrative medicine labs; less commonly ordered than total IgG panels.

clinical.name: Intestinal permeability testing (zonulin, lactulose/mannitol ratio)
clinical.description: Tests for 'leaky gut' — the proposed mechanism by which food proteins gain access to the immune system and stimulate IgG production. Serum zonulin (a tight-junction protein) can be measured via ELISA; the lactulose/mannitol urine test measures how much of each sugar crosses the gut lining after ingestion, with an elevated ratio suggesting increased permeability. Neither test is yet fully standardised, but both can help contextualise a broad IgG panel and inform treatment decisions.
clinical.availability: Zonulin via Cyrex, Genova Diagnostics, and similar labs; lactulose/mannitol through some functional medicine and gastroenterology practices. Rarely available through routine NHS or standard insurance pathways.

clinical.name: IgE allergy panel (to rule out IgE-mediated allergy)
clinical.description: A standard serum test (specific IgE, also called RAST or ImmunoCAP) or skin-prick test to identify true IgE-mediated food allergies, which cause immediate reactions. This is essential to conduct before or alongside IgG testing to distinguish delayed IgG-type sensitivity from immediate allergy — the two require very different clinical management.
clinical.availability: Widely available through allergists, immunologists, and many GPs. Typically covered by insurance/NHS when food allergy is suspected.

clinical.name: Comprehensive stool microbiome analysis
clinical.description: While not a test for IgG sensitivity itself, stool testing (e.g., Genova GI Effects, Viome, Diagnostic Solutions GI-MAP) can identify underlying gut dysbiosis, pathogens, low secretory IgA, or elevated inflammation markers (calprotectin, lactoferrin) that may be driving increased gut permeability and thus secondary IgG food reactions. Useful for understanding the upstream picture.
clinical.availability: Available through functional medicine practitioners and some gastroenterologists; direct-to-consumer options exist but results require professional interpretation.

clinical.name: Elimination diet guided by a FODMAP-trained or specialised dietitian
clinical.description: Structured dietary elimination under the guidance of a registered dietitian is considered the most clinically reliable method to identify food triggers — regardless of what any blood test shows. A skilled dietitian can design an elimination protocol, ensure nutritional adequacy, guide systematic reintroduction, and help distinguish IgG-related triggers from FODMAP, histamine, or other food chemical intolerances that may co-exist.
clinical.availability: Available via GP/physician referral or directly. Look specifically for dietitians trained in food sensitivity or elimination protocols — not all dietitians have this specialty.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: IgG food sensitivity and IBS share nearly identical gut symptom profiles. Several RCTs have tested IgG-guided elimination specifically in IBS populations and found meaningful symptom improvement, suggesting that in a subgroup of IBS patients, IgG food reactions may be a contributing driver rather than a coincidental finding.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: An imbalanced gut microbiome can compromise the integrity of the intestinal lining, increasing permeability and allowing food-derived antigens to stimulate IgG antibody production. Dysbiosis is often upstream of the IgG reactivity rather than a separate condition.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: SIBO can directly damage intestinal tight junctions and increase gut permeability, potentially driving secondary IgG food reactions. People with undiagnosed SIBO may find their IgG panel shows broad reactivity that does not resolve with food elimination alone until the SIBO is treated.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: MCAS causes delayed and unpredictable multi-system reactions to foods and other triggers, which can closely mimic IgG food sensitivity. The two conditions can co-exist, but MCAS has a distinct mast cell degranulation mechanism; IgG panels are not relevant to MCAS diagnosis, and some MCAS triggers (fermented foods, histamine-rich foods) are not captured on standard IgG tests.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine intolerance produces delayed multi-system symptoms (headache, flushing, gut upset, brain fog) that overlap substantially with IgG food sensitivity. Some foods that appear on IgG panels (wine, aged cheese, fermented foods) are also high-histamine foods, creating ambiguity about the actual mechanism.

related_conditions.name: Non-coeliac gluten sensitivity (NCGS)
related_conditions.explanation: NCGS involves immune and neurological reactions to gluten in the absence of coeliac disease autoantibodies or IgE allergy. IgG anti-gliadin antibodies (AGA-IgG) are elevated in a proportion of NCGS patients and may be one component of the immune response; however, NCGS diagnosis does not require IgG testing and the overlap between the two is imperfect.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease must be ruled out before pursuing IgG-based investigation of gluten reactions. Coeliac involves IgA (and IgG) anti-tissue transglutaminase antibodies and causes villous atrophy — a fundamentally different mechanism requiring strict lifelong exclusion. It is tested via tTG-IgA and EMA-IgA serology, not general IgG food panels.

related_conditions.name: Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
related_conditions.explanation: People with eczema frequently investigate IgG food sensitivities as a possible dietary trigger for flares. Research supports dietary triggers in a subset of eczema patients, and some individuals report meaningful skin improvement after IgG-guided elimination, particularly when avoiding dairy and eggs.

related_conditions.name: IgE-mediated allergy
related_conditions.explanation: True food allergy is IgE-mediated and causes immediate (within minutes) symptoms including urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis. IgG food sensitivity is distinct in mechanism, timing, and severity. The two can co-exist but require different investigation and management strategies.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Eosinophilic oesophagitis and eosinophilic gastroenteritis involve immune-mediated food reactions that are neither classic IgE nor IgG in mechanism, but produce delayed GI symptoms. They require endoscopic biopsy for diagnosis and are sometimes discovered in people who initially investigate IgG sensitivities.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Fibromyalgia and IgG food sensitivity share overlapping features — chronic widespread pain, fatigue, and brain fog. Some fibromyalgia patients report symptom improvement with dietary elimination, though the mechanism is unclear and the two conditions may co-exist independently.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation / visceral hypersensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic immune activation from food reactions may contribute to central sensitisation over time, lowering pain thresholds and amplifying symptom perception. Conversely, central sensitisation can make it difficult to distinguish true dietary triggers from noise — the nervous system is more reactive to all stimuli.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Upper GI symptoms such as nausea, early satiety, and epigastric discomfort attributed to functional dyspepsia may in some cases have dietary triggers identifiable via IgG testing or elimination, though the research base is less developed than in IBS.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic, unexplained multi-system symptoms often co-occur with anxiety, and the relationship is bidirectional: gut immune activation can worsen anxiety via the gut-brain axis, and anxiety amplifies symptom perception and gut permeability. Managing anxiety is often part of a complete treatment picture.

related_conditions.name: Thyroid dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Hashimoto's thyroiditis in particular is associated with intestinal permeability and immune dysregulation. Gluten IgG reactivity is more prevalent in autoimmune thyroid disease, and some patients with Hashimoto's report improved thyroid antibody levels or symptom burden with gluten elimination.

related_conditions.name: hEDS / HSD (hypermobile EDS)
related_conditions.explanation: People with hypermobile EDS frequently report food sensitivities and GI dysmotility. Connective tissue abnormalities in the gut wall may contribute to increased permeability and immune reactivity to food antigens.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: POTS and IgG food sensitivity co-occur in individuals with connective tissue disorders and mast cell activation. Post-meal symptom exacerbation in POTS is common, and some POTS patients find that reducing reactive foods decreases their overall symptom burden.

strategies.name: IgG-guided elimination diet
strategies.description: Remove foods flagged as high or moderate reactors on an IgG panel for 4–8 weeks, then reintroduce systematically one at a time every 3–5 days, monitoring symptoms carefully. The elimination phase aims to reduce immune load and allow gut healing; the reintroduction phase identifies which foods are genuinely symptomatic versus incidentally flagged.
strategies.notes: Works best when directed by a registered dietitian to ensure nutritional adequacy. IgG results should be treated as a hypothesis-generating tool, not a definitive prescription — reintroduction findings override panel results.

strategies.name: Rotation diet for reactive foods
strategies.description: Rather than permanently eliminating all reactive foods, rotate them so that no single food is eaten more than once every 4 days. This may reduce cumulative immune load and allow some tolerance to build over time, particularly for moderate-reactivity foods.
strategies.notes: Widely recommended in functional medicine circles; limited RCT evidence but mechanistically plausible for reducing antigen exposure frequency.

strategies.name: Gut lining repair protocol
strategies.description: Addressing intestinal permeability directly may reduce the volume of reactive foods over time. Common approaches include L-glutamine (an amino acid that supports enterocyte integrity), zinc carnosine, collagen/bone broth, and deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL). These are often combined with removal of gut-damaging factors (NSAIDs, alcohol, stress).
strategies.notes: L-glutamine has reasonable mechanistic support and some clinical evidence for intestinal repair. Full protocols should be supervised by a functional medicine doctor or integrative practitioner. This addresses the upstream driver rather than just avoiding trigger foods.

strategies.name: Probiotic and prebiotic support
strategies.description: A diverse gut microbiome supports tight junction integrity and immune regulation. Specific probiotic strains (Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG, Lactobacillus reuteri, Bifidobacterium species) have evidence for reducing intestinal permeability. Prebiotic fibre feeds beneficial bacteria and may help over time.
strategies.notes: Probiotic response is highly individual. People with SIBO should not start high-dose probiotics without ruling out or treating SIBO first, as symptoms can worsen.

strategies.name: NSAID and alcohol reduction
strategies.description: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin) and alcohol are well-established causes of intestinal permeability. Chronic or regular use significantly increases gut leakiness and may be a primary driver of a broad IgG food reaction pattern. Reducing or eliminating these compounds may be more impactful than any supplement.
strategies.notes: People using NSAIDs regularly for joint pain or headaches may find their food sensitivity improves substantially when they switch to alternative pain management. This requires addressing the underlying pain driver.

strategies.name: Stress management and nervous system regulation
strategies.description: Chronic psychological stress increases gut permeability via corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF) and directly activates mast cells in the gut lining. Stress regulation practices — including diaphragmatic breathing, HRV biofeedback, mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR), and trauma-informed approaches — may reduce immune reactivity to foods by lowering baseline physiological threat response.
strategies.notes: Often underestimated as a lever. This does not mean symptoms are 'just stress' — it means the immune system is genuinely affected by the nervous system state.

strategies.name: Digestive enzyme support
strategies.description: Thorough digestion of food proteins before they reach the gut wall may reduce the volume of intact antigens available to stimulate IgG reactions. Broad-spectrum digestive enzymes (including protease, amylase, lipase) taken with meals may help some people, particularly those with low stomach acid or sluggish pancreatic output.
strategies.notes: Most useful in people with signs of poor digestion (undigested food in stool, post-meal heaviness, early satiety). Less likely to be the primary driver for most people.

strategies.name: Quercetin
strategies.description: A polyphenol found in onions, apples, and berries, quercetin has demonstrated mast cell stabilising effects and some evidence for supporting tight junction protein expression. May help reduce gut immune reactivity in addition to dietary changes.
strategies.notes: Most evidence is from in vitro or animal studies; human RCT evidence is limited but growing. Generally well tolerated.

strategies.name: Low-inflammatory whole-food dietary base
strategies.description: Regardless of specific trigger foods, an overall dietary pattern rich in diverse vegetables, polyphenols, omega-3 fatty acids, and low in ultra-processed foods reduces gut inflammation and supports microbiome diversity. This creates a more favourable immune environment that may reduce reactivity over time.
strategies.notes: Often the most sustainable and broadly beneficial intervention — works through multiple mechanisms simultaneously and does not require ongoing restriction of specific foods.

things_to_avoid: Eliminating foods permanently based on IgG results alone without a reintroduction phase — this leads to unnecessary lifelong restriction and nutritional gaps
Treating all IgG-flagged foods as equally problematic — high reactors warrant elimination first; moderate reactors can often be rotated rather than eliminated
Continuing regular NSAID use while trying to heal gut permeability — these drugs directly and reproducibly increase intestinal leakiness
Eliminating 20+ foods simultaneously without dietitian guidance — this is nutritionally unsustainable and makes reintroduction extremely difficult
Relying on IgG testing alone without ruling out coeliac disease (which requires active gluten consumption at the time of testing)
Pursuing IgG elimination without addressing upstream causes (SIBO, dysbiosis, stress) — symptoms often return because the driver is still present
Interpreting a negative IgG panel as proof that food is not involved — testing has real false-negative rates and elimination remains the most reliable diagnostic tool
Using elimination as a long-term anxiety-management strategy without working toward reintroduction — this can develop into a restrictive relationship with food that causes its own harm

uncomfortable_truths.truth: IgG food testing is not validated as a diagnostic test, and most mainstream doctors and allergists will tell you the results are meaningless. The science genuinely does not yet support using IgG panels as definitive evidence of food sensitivity.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People who have spent money on IgG testing and found it useful want to believe the results are reliable. Dismissing the test feels like dismissing their experience — especially when they have already improved after following the results.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: The test result itself is less important than the structured elimination and reintroduction process it prompts. Patients who treat IgG results as a starting hypothesis, confirm findings through reintroduction, and work with a dietitian to identify their true minimal trigger set consistently get more useful, lasting answers than those who either believe the results uncritically or dismiss them entirely.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: A broad IgG panel showing reactivity to 30+ foods is rarely a list of 30 foods to avoid. It is more likely a sign of intestinal permeability — meaning the gut lining is letting too much in — and restricting all those foods will not fix the underlying problem.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Elimination feels like action and control. Accepting that the real problem is gut barrier dysfunction requires a longer, less clear treatment path and often implicates lifestyle factors (stress, alcohol, medication use) that are harder to change than diet.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who focus on repairing gut permeability — treating SIBO if present, reducing gut-damaging medications, supporting the microbiome, managing stress — often find that their reactive food list shrinks significantly over 6–12 months and they can tolerate foods they previously could not.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Elimination diets can themselves become a problem. Prolonged, overly restrictive diets can cause nutritional deficiencies, worsen the relationship with food, increase food-related anxiety, and paradoxically reduce microbiome diversity in ways that worsen gut health.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: When restriction brings symptom relief, it feels like proof that the eliminated foods were harmful. The idea that the restriction itself might eventually become harmful — or that tolerance can be rebuilt — can feel threatening to a hard-won sense of safety around food.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who work systematically through reintroduction, identify their actual minimal trigger set (usually 2–5 foods rather than 20+), and then maintain a broad, diverse diet outside those specific triggers typically have better long-term outcomes — nutritionally, symptomatically, and psychologically — than those who remain in permanent broad restriction.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Stress, sleep deprivation, and psychological trauma directly increase gut permeability and immune reactivity to food. For some people, these are the primary drivers — and no amount of dietary restriction will produce lasting improvement until these are addressed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Being told that stress is involved in a physical, immune-mediated condition still feels to many people like being told it is 'all in their head.' It can also feel overwhelming because stress and trauma are harder to change than diet.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Patients who address nervous system regulation alongside dietary changes — particularly through evidence-based approaches like MBSR, HRV biofeedback, or trauma therapy — often find that their dietary tolerance improves and their reactive food list shrinks in ways that dietary intervention alone did not produce.

key_levers.lever: Structured elimination and reintroduction to identify the true minimal trigger set
key_levers.why_it_matters: Most people self-restricting based on IgG results are avoiding more foods than necessary, which is unsustainable and reduces gut microbiome diversity. Identifying the actual symptomatic foods — typically a small subset of what the panel flags — allows targeted restriction rather than broad dietary impoverishment.
key_levers.how_to_start: Work with a registered dietitian experienced in food sensitivity or elimination protocols. Begin with the highest-reactivity flagged foods and the most plausible common triggers (gluten, dairy, eggs). Run a clean 4–6 week elimination, then reintroduce methodically.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks for elimination phase; 8–16 additional weeks for full reintroduction of priority foods

key_levers.lever: Identify and treat upstream gut drivers (SIBO, dysbiosis, H. pylori, parasites)
key_levers.why_it_matters: If a gut pathogen or overgrowth is driving intestinal permeability, food elimination is a symptomatic patch that will not produce lasting improvement. Treating the upstream cause often resolves the secondary food reactivity without ongoing dietary restriction.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request or arrange a breath test for SIBO, a comprehensive stool test including pathogens and microbiome markers, and H. pylori testing if upper GI symptoms are present. A functional medicine doctor or well-informed gastroenterologist can help direct this.
key_levers.timeline: Investigation and treatment: 3–6 months; improvement in food tolerance may follow over 3–12 months as the gut heals

key_levers.why_it_matters: If the gut lining is the entry point for food antigens triggering IgG reactions, repairing it reduces the antigenic load regardless of which specific foods are eaten. This is the closest thing to addressing the root mechanism available without specialised intervention.
key_levers.how_to_start: Remove gut-damaging inputs first (regular NSAIDs, excess alcohol, ultra-processed foods). Then introduce gut-supportive nutrients: L-glutamine (3–10g daily in water), zinc carnosine, diverse prebiotic fibre, and a clinician-selected probiotic. Collagen-rich foods or supplements may also help.
key_levers.timeline: Measurable improvement in gut integrity: 8–16 weeks with consistent implementation

key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic stress is a direct physiological cause of gut permeability via the HPA-gut axis. No dietary protocol will achieve its full potential if the nervous system remains in a chronic threat state. This lever often produces the most durable improvements in overall food tolerance.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose one evidence-based practice: a structured 8-week MBSR course, daily HRV biofeedback (5–10 minutes using a validated device/app), or begin working with a trauma-informed therapist if adverse life events are relevant history.
key_levers.timeline: Perceptible nervous system change: 6–12 weeks of consistent practice; meaningful reduction in gut reactivity: 3–6 months

key_levers.why_it_matters: Regular NSAID use (ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin at anti-inflammatory doses) is one of the most potent causes of intestinal permeability. Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) alter gut microbiome composition. These medications may be silently perpetuating the condition regardless of dietary changes.
key_levers.how_to_start: Audit all regular medications with a GP or prescribing doctor. Explore alternative pain management for conditions driving regular NSAID use (physiotherapy, topical diclofenac, paracetamol/acetaminophen). Never stop prescribed medications without medical guidance.
key_levers.timeline: Gut lining begins recovering within weeks of NSAID reduction; meaningful change in food tolerance typically takes 2–4 months

common_mistakes: Acting on an IgG panel without completing a structured reintroduction phase — this leaves people with an unnecessarily restricted diet and no validated trigger list
Pursuing dietary elimination without investigating upstream causes — the food reactions are often downstream of SIBO, dysbiosis, or gut permeability issues that elimination alone cannot resolve
Treating IgG results as permanent — most food reactions identified by IgG testing can be resolved or reduced once gut permeability and upstream drivers are addressed

Self-managing with a very broad elimination diet without dietitian oversight — nutritional inadequacy (particularly calcium, vitamin D, B vitamins, fibre) accumulates over months and creates new health problems
Testing before ruling out coeliac disease while eating gluten — coeliac serology requires active gluten consumption; if someone has eliminated gluten before testing, results will be falsely negative
Choosing a laboratory for IgG testing based on cost alone — methodological variation between labs is substantial; results from different labs for the same patient can differ significantly
Focusing only on food and ignoring environmental toxins, medications, and stress — in complex or broad-reactivity cases, these non-dietary drivers are often as important as the food triggers

specialists.role: The most directly useful practitioner for implementing an IgG-guided or elimination-based dietary investigation. A dietitian with this specialty can design a nutritionally adequate elimination protocol, guide systematic reintroduction, help distinguish IgG triggers from FODMAP or histamine issues, and prevent the over-restriction that commonly develops when people self-manage.
specialists.notes: Look specifically for dietitians who list food sensitivity, elimination diets, or IBS as a specialty area. FODMAP-trained dietitians often have the closest relevant skill set. Many mainstream dietitians are sceptical of IgG testing — what matters is finding one who is willing to support structured dietary investigation regardless of their view on the test itself.

specialists.role: Trained to investigate root causes of chronic symptoms including gut permeability, dysbiosis, SIBO, nutrient deficiencies, and hormonal drivers. Typically the most willing to order and interpret IgG panels alongside other gut-health markers, and to supervise a comprehensive repair protocol.
specialists.notes: Quality and approach varies significantly. Look for practitioners certified through the Institute for Functional Medicine (IFM) or equivalent. Be aware that some functional medicine practitioners run expensive panel batteries not all of which are necessary — a good one will prioritise systematically.

specialists.role: Often experienced in dietary elimination approaches, gut-healing protocols, and supplement-based interventions. May be more willing than mainstream practitioners to engage with IgG test results and design a comprehensive treatment plan.
specialists.notes: Regulation and training standards vary significantly by country and jurisdiction. In the US, licensed naturopathic doctors (NDs) in regulated states have a comparable training duration to medical doctors with an integrative focus; in unregulated regions, credentials vary more widely.

specialists.role: Important for ruling out structural and organic conditions (coeliac disease, IBD, microscopic colitis, eosinophilic disorders) before pursuing IgG-based investigation. Most mainstream gastroenterologists will not order or act on IgG panels but are essential for structural investigation.
specialists.notes: Mainstream gastroenterologists typically manage IBS symptomatically and may dismiss IgG testing. An integrative or functional gastroenterologist, if available, combines the ability to rule out structural causes with willingness to investigate immune and microbiome drivers.

specialists.role: Essential for ruling out IgE-mediated food allergy (which requires fundamentally different management) and for identifying co-existing conditions like MCAS or eosinophilic GI disorders. Most allergists will actively discourage IgG testing, but their role in ruling out IgE mechanisms is important.
specialists.notes: The allergist's scepticism about IgG testing should not discourage investigation of delayed food reactions — it should be used to ensure the right conditions are being ruled out first.

patient_communities.name: r/FoodSensitivities
patient_communities.notes: Active community of people investigating and managing food sensitivities including IgG reactions. Useful for hearing real patient experiences with different testing labs, elimination protocols, and practitioners.

patient_communities.name: r/Elimination_Diet
patient_communities.notes: Focused specifically on elimination diet experiences, meal ideas, and protocol support. Helpful for practical implementation questions.

patient_communities.name: r/Leaky Gut
patient_communities.notes: Community discussing intestinal permeability as a driver of food sensitivities and multi-system symptoms. Useful for understanding the upstream gut health picture.

patient_communities.name: r/functionalmedicine
patient_communities.notes: Broader community discussing root-cause approaches to chronic conditions including food sensitivities. Useful for practitioner recommendations and protocol discussions.

patient_communities.name: Food Sensitivity Community (various groups)
patient_communities.notes: Multiple Facebook groups exist around IgG testing and food sensitivity — search 'IgG food sensitivity' or 'food intolerance support'. Quality varies; look for groups with active moderation and evidence-informed moderators.

learning_resources.title: The Gut-Brain Connection: How Leaky Gut Drives Food Sensitivity
learning_resources.author_or_source: Fasano A. (various papers on intestinal permeability and tight junction regulation)
learning_resources.description: Alessio Fasano's research on zonulin and intestinal permeability provides the best current scientific framework for understanding how a compromised gut lining leads to immune reactions to food proteins. His papers are accessible via PubMed and form the mechanistic basis for the leaky-gut-drives-IgG-reactivity hypothesis.

learning_resources.title: IgG-guided exclusion diet significantly reduces symptoms in irritable bowel syndrome: a 12-week intervention study (Atkinson et al., 2004, Gut)
learning_resources.description: The landmark RCT that found IgG-guided elimination significantly outperformed a sham diet in IBS patients, providing the strongest clinical evidence for the approach. Essential reading for anyone trying to understand the evidence base — and its limitations.

learning_resources.title: Practical Guide to Elimination Diets
learning_resources.author_or_source: Monash University / various FODMAP-trained dietitian resources
learning_resources.description: While primarily focused on the low-FODMAP protocol, Monash University's resources on elimination and reintroduction methodology are the gold standard for understanding how to conduct a dietary investigation properly. The principles apply directly to IgG-guided elimination.

learning_resources.title: The Elimination Diet: Discover the Foods That Are Making You Sick and Tired — and Feel Better Fast
learning_resources.description: A practical, well-structured guide to implementing an elimination diet with recipes and a clear protocol. Accessible for beginners and grounded in functional nutrition principles. Useful as a companion to working with a practitioner.

learning_resources.title: Ultra-processed food, dietary patterns, and gut permeability: a review
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various — search PubMed for 'ultra-processed food intestinal permeability'
learning_resources.description: Emerging research connecting ultra-processed food consumption with gut barrier dysfunction — useful context for understanding why dietary quality affects the overall food sensitivity picture, not just specific trigger foods.

learning_resources.title: Food Sensitivity Testing: What the Research Really Shows
learning_resources.description: Examine.com provides evidence-graded summaries of research on food, supplements, and health claims without commercial bias. Searching for IgG food testing, gut permeability, L-glutamine, and related topics gives a useful, balanced picture of what is and is not well-supported.

learning_resources.title: Accessing Your Gut Health: Understanding and Using IgG Food Sensitivity Testing
learning_resources.author_or_source: The Dhru Purohit Podcast / Found My Fitness (Rhonda Patrick) — various episodes on gut health and food immunity
learning_resources.description: Several long-form podcasts have featured researchers and clinicians discussing IgG food reactions, leaky gut, and dietary intervention in the context of chronic disease. Searching these platforms for 'food sensitivity', 'leaky gut', or 'intestinal permeability' yields accessible, nuanced expert discussions.

sections.relevance_signal: **Delayed reactions 4–72 hours after eating** — hard to link to a specific food. Symptoms include bloating, fatigue, skin issues, and brain fog with no clear immediate trigger.
